passage_text: When the old post office in Aremo village was converted into a public library, many residents thought the project would fail within a few months. The building had a leaking roof, the shelves were uneven and the first collection of books looked too small to attract serious readers. Yet the youth corps members who supervised the renovation believed that the library could become more than a room filled with dusty pages. They organized evening reading circles, invited retired teachers to tell stories and set up a corner where farmers could read pamphlets on soil improvement and crop storage. At first, children came mainly for the free biscuits sometimes shared after the reading sessions. Before long, however, they began to compete over who would finish a novel first. Adults who had not entered a classroom in years started to use the library to fill forms, check agricultural notices and prepare for professional examinations. The library did not solve every problem in the village, but it quietly changed what many people thought education was for.
question: The passage is mainly about how the library

options.A: failed because the building was too old
options.B: became useful by changing how villagers viewed education
options.C: was built by retired teachers alone
options.D: was meant only for children who liked novels

answer_text: became useful by changing how villagers viewed education

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the central message running through the entire passage.
explanation.principle: The main idea is the broad point that ties together the opening problem, the middle details and the final comment.
steps: The passage begins with doubt about the project.
It then lists practical activities that widened the library's usefulness.
It ends by saying the library changed what many people thought education was for.
So the deepest unifying idea is not merely that a library existed, but that it reshaped attitudes to education in the village.
explanation.final_answer: B. became useful by changing how villagers viewed education
explanation.simple_explanation: The library matters because it changed minds about what education can do, not because it was a perfect building. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: When the old post office in Aremo village was converted into a public library, many residents thought the project would fail within a few months. The building had a leaking roof, the shelves were uneven and the first collection of books looked too small to attract serious readers. Yet the youth corps members who supervised the renovation believed that the library could become more than a room filled with dusty pages. They organized evening reading circles, invited retired teachers to tell stories and set up a corner where farmers could read pamphlets on soil improvement and crop storage. At first, children came mainly for the free biscuits sometimes shared after the reading sessions. Before long, however, they began to compete over who would finish a novel first. Adults who had not entered a classroom in years started to use the library to fill forms, check agricultural notices and prepare for professional examinations. The library did not solve every problem in the village, but it quietly changed what many people thought education was for.
question: Which of the following is mentioned as one of the ways the library was made useful?

options.A: It was turned into a cinema hall at weekends
options.B: Farmers were taught only by visiting scientists
options.C: A section was provided for pamphlets on farming
options.D: Students were forced by law to register there

answer_text: A section was provided for pamphlets on farming

explanation.question_restate: We are to pick a detail directly stated in the passage.
explanation.principle: Supporting details are specific pieces of information the writer uses to develop the main point.
steps: The passage says the organizers set up a corner where farmers could read pamphlets on soil improvement and crop storage.
That statement matches option C exactly.
The other options introduce ideas not stated in the passage.
A good comprehension student separates what is written from what merely sounds possible.
explanation.final_answer: C. A section was provided for pamphlets on farming
explanation.simple_explanation: One correct detail can often be lifted almost word-for-word from the passage. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: When the old post office in Aremo village was converted into a public library, many residents thought the project would fail within a few months. The building had a leaking roof, the shelves were uneven and the first collection of books looked too small to attract serious readers. Yet the youth corps members who supervised the renovation believed that the library could become more than a room filled with dusty pages. They organized evening reading circles, invited retired teachers to tell stories and set up a corner where farmers could read pamphlets on soil improvement and crop storage. At first, children came mainly for the free biscuits sometimes shared after the reading sessions. Before long, however, they began to compete over who would finish a novel first. Adults who had not entered a classroom in years started to use the library to fill forms, check agricultural notices and prepare for professional examinations. The library did not solve every problem in the village, but it quietly changed what many people thought education was for.
question: It can be inferred from the passage that the villagers' earlier understanding of education was mostly

options.A: broad and community-centred
options.B: limited and narrowly academic
options.C: hostile to all forms of reading
options.D: based on modern technology

answer_text: limited and narrowly academic

explanation.question_restate: We are to read beyond the direct words and infer what the villagers probably believed before the library succeeded.
explanation.principle: Inference requires combining clues instead of waiting for one sentence to state the answer openly.
steps: The final line says the library changed what many people thought education was for.
Adults later used it to fill forms, read notices and prepare for examinations.
Farmers used it for practical agricultural information.
These clues suggest that before the library's success, many people probably saw education in a narrower, more classroom-bound way.
explanation.final_answer: B. limited and narrowly academic
explanation.simple_explanation: The writer implies that people first had a limited idea of education, but experience broadened it. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Contextual meaning of words and expressions
passage_text: When the old post office in Aremo village was converted into a public library, many residents thought the project would fail within a few months. The building had a leaking roof, the shelves were uneven and the first collection of books looked too small to attract serious readers. Yet the youth corps members who supervised the renovation believed that the library could become more than a room filled with dusty pages. They organized evening reading circles, invited retired teachers to tell stories and set up a corner where farmers could read pamphlets on soil improvement and crop storage. At first, children came mainly for the free biscuits sometimes shared after the reading sessions. Before long, however, they began to compete over who would finish a novel first. Adults who had not entered a classroom in years started to use the library to fill forms, check agricultural notices and prepare for professional examinations. The library did not solve every problem in the village, but it quietly changed what many people thought education was for.
question: In the passage, the expression "quietly changed" suggests that the library changed opinions

options.A: through violence and public protest
options.B: suddenly and noisily
options.C: gradually and without dramatic display
options.D: secretly and dishonestly

answer_text: gradually and without dramatic display

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret an expression as it is used in the passage, not in isolation.
explanation.principle: Contextual meaning depends on the surrounding idea and tone.
steps: The writer describes a slow process: children first came for biscuits, adults later found practical uses, and opinions changed over time.
Nothing in the passage suggests noise, force or dishonesty.
Therefore, "quietly changed" means the change happened gently and gradually rather than through dramatic events.
explanation.final_answer: C. gradually and without dramatic display
explanation.simple_explanation: Context tells us that the change was real but not flashy. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: When the old post office in Aremo village was converted into a public library, many residents thought the project would fail within a few months. The building had a leaking roof, the shelves were uneven and the first collection of books looked too small to attract serious readers. Yet the youth corps members who supervised the renovation believed that the library could become more than a room filled with dusty pages. They organized evening reading circles, invited retired teachers to tell stories and set up a corner where farmers could read pamphlets on soil improvement and crop storage. At first, children came mainly for the free biscuits sometimes shared after the reading sessions. Before long, however, they began to compete over who would finish a novel first. Adults who had not entered a classroom in years started to use the library to fill forms, check agricultural notices and prepare for professional examinations. The library did not solve every problem in the village, but it quietly changed what many people thought education was for.
question: The writer's attitude to the library project is best described as

options.A: cautiously approving
options.B: openly hostile
options.C: carelessly indifferent
options.D: mockingly amused

answer_text: cautiously approving

explanation.question_restate: We are to determine the writer's tone toward the project.
explanation.principle: Tone is the writer's emotional posture, shown by word choice and overall treatment.
steps: The passage acknowledges the building's poor condition and the initial doubts surrounding it.
Yet it steadily presents evidence of useful impact on children, adults and farmers.
The final sentence is approving, but the writer does not exaggerate by saying the library solved every problem.
That balanced praise shows cautious approval rather than blind excitement.
explanation.final_answer: A. cautiously approving
explanation.simple_explanation: The writer clearly supports the project, but in a measured and realistic way. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: At the Federal College campus, students complain loudly whenever the taps run dry, yet few notice the rainwater that disappears from the roofs after every storm. Last year, the environmental club proposed that each hostel should channel roof water into storage tanks for cleaning, gardening and emergency use. Some students laughed at the idea and called it a poor substitute for government responsibility. The club members replied that conserving water was not an attempt to excuse official failure but a practical response to an urgent need. After a pilot scheme was installed in one hostel, the benefits became visible. The hostel used less treated water for washing corridors, the vegetable garden behind the cafeteria remained green during a brief shortage, and maintenance workers reported fewer quarrels at the public taps. The project did not end all scarcity, but it showed that careful planning can reduce waste. What had looked like a desperate improvisation gradually appeared to be an intelligent habit.
question: The writer's main purpose in the passage is to

options.A: mock students who live in hostels
options.B: show that a practical conservation idea can reduce water waste
options.C: argue that government should never provide water
options.D: prove that only environmental clubs understand science

answer_text: show that a practical conservation idea can reduce water waste

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify why the writer presented this passage.
explanation.principle: Writer's purpose asks what the passage is trying to achieve in the reader's mind.
steps: The passage starts with water shortage and unnoticed rainwater waste.
It then describes a proposal, opposition to it, and the visible benefits of a pilot scheme.
The final sentence confirms the message: what looked desperate later seemed sensible.
So the writer's aim is to show the value of practical conservation.
explanation.final_answer: B. show that a practical conservation idea can reduce water waste
explanation.simple_explanation: The passage tries to persuade the reader that sensible local action can ease a real problem. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: At the Federal College campus, students complain loudly whenever the taps run dry, yet few notice the rainwater that disappears from the roofs after every storm. Last year, the environmental club proposed that each hostel should channel roof water into storage tanks for cleaning, gardening and emergency use. Some students laughed at the idea and called it a poor substitute for government responsibility. The club members replied that conserving water was not an attempt to excuse official failure but a practical response to an urgent need. After a pilot scheme was installed in one hostel, the benefits became visible. The hostel used less treated water for washing corridors, the vegetable garden behind the cafeteria remained green during a brief shortage, and maintenance workers reported fewer quarrels at the public taps. The project did not end all scarcity, but it showed that careful planning can reduce waste. What had looked like a desperate improvisation gradually appeared to be an intelligent habit.
question: According to the passage, the pilot rainwater scheme reduced quarrels at the public taps because it

options.A: forced students to bathe less often
options.B: provided an additional source for some hostel uses
options.C: closed the public taps completely
options.D: made rainfall more frequent on campus

answer_text: provided an additional source for some hostel uses

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the cause that led to a stated effect.
explanation.principle: Cause-and-effect questions require you to link an action in the passage to the result that followed from it.
steps: The passage says roof water was stored for cleaning, gardening and emergency use.
Because some needs were met from stored rainwater, the hostel used less treated water.
That reduced pressure on the normal water supply and helped reduce quarrels at the taps.
The effect came from better resource management, not from magic or weather change.
explanation.final_answer: B. provided an additional source for some hostel uses
explanation.simple_explanation: When demand falls because another source is available, conflict often reduces. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: At the Federal College campus, students complain loudly whenever the taps run dry, yet few notice the rainwater that disappears from the roofs after every storm. Last year, the environmental club proposed that each hostel should channel roof water into storage tanks for cleaning, gardening and emergency use. Some students laughed at the idea and called it a poor substitute for government responsibility. The club members replied that conserving water was not an attempt to excuse official failure but a practical response to an urgent need. After a pilot scheme was installed in one hostel, the benefits became visible. The hostel used less treated water for washing corridors, the vegetable garden behind the cafeteria remained green during a brief shortage, and maintenance workers reported fewer quarrels at the public taps. The project did not end all scarcity, but it showed that careful planning can reduce waste. What had looked like a desperate improvisation gradually appeared to be an intelligent habit.
question: Which of the following statements is an opinion expressed in the passage?

options.A: The project was installed in one hostel first
options.B: Maintenance workers reported fewer quarrels at public taps
options.C: The idea was a poor substitute for government responsibility
options.D: The hostel used less treated water for washing corridors

answer_text: The idea was a poor substitute for government responsibility

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish between a verifiable statement and a judgment.
explanation.principle: A fact can be checked; an opinion reflects belief, feeling or evaluation.
steps: Option C presents a critical judgment made by some students.
It is not a measurable fact in itself; it expresses a viewpoint about the proposal.
The other options report events or outcomes that the passage presents as observable.
Opinion often appears in evaluative language such as "poor substitute."
explanation.final_answer: C. The idea was a poor substitute for government responsibility
explanation.simple_explanation: That statement shows attitude, not an objective record. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: At the Federal College campus, students complain loudly whenever the taps run dry, yet few notice the rainwater that disappears from the roofs after every storm. Last year, the environmental club proposed that each hostel should channel roof water into storage tanks for cleaning, gardening and emergency use. Some students laughed at the idea and called it a poor substitute for government responsibility. The club members replied that conserving water was not an attempt to excuse official failure but a practical response to an urgent need. After a pilot scheme was installed in one hostel, the benefits became visible. The hostel used less treated water for washing corridors, the vegetable garden behind the cafeteria remained green during a brief shortage, and maintenance workers reported fewer quarrels at the public taps. The project did not end all scarcity, but it showed that careful planning can reduce waste. What had looked like a desperate improvisation gradually appeared to be an intelligent habit.
question: In the sentence, "The club members replied that conserving water was not an attempt to excuse official failure but a practical response to an urgent need," the word "it" would most naturally refer to

options.A: the rainwater harvesting idea
options.B: official failure
options.C: the vegetable garden
options.D: the public taps

answer_text: the rainwater harvesting idea

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the noun a reference expression points back to.
explanation.principle: Reference words help avoid repetition, so you must trace them to the most logical earlier idea.
steps: The passage centres on the proposal to collect rainwater from hostel roofs.
When later sentences discuss whether the project was useful, the implied "it" refers to that proposal or scheme.
The other options are nearby ideas but not the main item being evaluated.
In comprehension, always ask: what is the sentence currently talking about?
explanation.final_answer: A. the rainwater harvesting idea
explanation.simple_explanation: The pronoun points back to the rainwater scheme under discussion. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: At the Federal College campus, students complain loudly whenever the taps run dry, yet few notice the rainwater that disappears from the roofs after every storm. Last year, the environmental club proposed that each hostel should channel roof water into storage tanks for cleaning, gardening and emergency use. Some students laughed at the idea and called it a poor substitute for government responsibility. The club members replied that conserving water was not an attempt to excuse official failure but a practical response to an urgent need. After a pilot scheme was installed in one hostel, the benefits became visible. The hostel used less treated water for washing corridors, the vegetable garden behind the cafeteria remained green during a brief shortage, and maintenance workers reported fewer quarrels at the public taps. The project did not end all scarcity, but it showed that careful planning can reduce waste. What had looked like a desperate improvisation gradually appeared to be an intelligent habit.
question: A sound conclusion from the passage is that the writer believes water shortages on campus can be

options.A: solved only by waiting for the government
options.B: reduced when institutions combine planning with available resources
options.C: ended entirely by one hostel project
options.D: ignored because rain falls often

answer_text: reduced when institutions combine planning with available resources

explanation.question_restate: We are to draw a reasonable conclusion from the whole passage.
explanation.principle: A conclusion is broader than a detail but must still grow naturally from the evidence given.
steps: The pilot scheme did not eliminate all scarcity, according to the writer.
However, it reduced waste and eased some pressure points.
This supports the conclusion that thoughtful use of available resources can reduce a problem even when it does not remove it completely.
Options A, C and D are extreme or careless and do not match the passage's balanced message.
explanation.final_answer: B. reduced when institutions combine planning with available resources
explanation.simple_explanation: Good conclusions are moderate, evidence-based and faithful to the passage's limits. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: Sade joined Madam Bisi's tailoring shop because her family needed extra income, but she soon discovered that sewing demanded more discipline than she had imagined. Needles had to be arranged carefully, measurements recorded accurately and fabrics cut with patience rather than excitement. In her first week, she spoiled an expensive Ankara material by trimming the sleeve too short. Expecting a harsh rebuke, she stood silently beside the cutting table. Madam Bisi only asked her to explain what she had done and why she had rushed. Then she made Sade use a leftover piece of cloth to practise sleeve patterns until closing time. Months later, customers began to request Sade's finishing because her stitches were neat and her buttonholes sat evenly. She still worked slowly, but she no longer mistook speed for skill. The shop taught her more than a trade. It showed her that embarrassment can become instruction when pride does not prevent learning.
question: Which statement best expresses the controlling idea of the passage?

options.A: Tailoring requires discipline, and mistakes can become part of learning
options.B: Customers always prefer neat buttonholes to fast service
options.C: Family poverty is the only reason apprentices enter tailoring
options.D: Ankara material is the hardest fabric to cut

answer_text: Tailoring requires discipline, and mistakes can become part of learning

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the sentence that could serve as the best topic sentence for the passage.
explanation.principle: A topic sentence states the main controlling idea around which other details are arranged.
steps: The passage begins with Sade joining the shop and discovering that sewing demands discipline.
Her mistake, correction and later growth all develop that same idea.
Option A captures both discipline and learning through error, which the passage strongly emphasizes.
The other options isolate minor details or exaggerate one part of the story.
explanation.final_answer: A. Tailoring requires discipline, and mistakes can become part of learning
explanation.simple_explanation: A strong topic sentence covers the whole passage, not just one incident. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: Sade joined Madam Bisi's tailoring shop because her family needed extra income, but she soon discovered that sewing demanded more discipline than she had imagined. Needles had to be arranged carefully, measurements recorded accurately and fabrics cut with patience rather than excitement. In her first week, she spoiled an expensive Ankara material by trimming the sleeve too short. Expecting a harsh rebuke, she stood silently beside the cutting table. Madam Bisi only asked her to explain what she had done and why she had rushed. Then she made Sade use a leftover piece of cloth to practise sleeve patterns until closing time. Months later, customers began to request Sade's finishing because her stitches were neat and her buttonholes sat evenly. She still worked slowly, but she no longer mistook speed for skill. The shop taught her more than a trade. It showed her that embarrassment can become instruction when pride does not prevent learning.
question: From Madam Bisi's treatment of Sade after the mistake, it can be deduced that Madam Bisi believes training is most effective when it

options.A: humiliates the learner publicly
options.B: replaces correction with praise only
options.C: turns mistakes into guided practice
options.D: ends immediately after an error

answer_text: turns mistakes into guided practice

explanation.question_restate: We are to deduce a principle from a character's action.
explanation.principle: Deduction involves reasoning from evidence toward a likely underlying belief or rule.
steps: Madam Bisi did not merely scold Sade.
She asked what happened and then made her practise sleeve patterns on leftover cloth.
That response shows she sees error as something to correct through supervised repetition.
So her training method is practical, corrective and instructional, not humiliating.
explanation.final_answer: C. turns mistakes into guided practice
explanation.simple_explanation: The passage suggests that real teaching does not ignore mistakes; it uses them wisely. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: Sade joined Madam Bisi's tailoring shop because her family needed extra income, but she soon discovered that sewing demanded more discipline than she had imagined. Needles had to be arranged carefully, measurements recorded accurately and fabrics cut with patience rather than excitement. In her first week, she spoiled an expensive Ankara material by trimming the sleeve too short. Expecting a harsh rebuke, she stood silently beside the cutting table. Madam Bisi only asked her to explain what she had done and why she had rushed. Then she made Sade use a leftover piece of cloth to practise sleeve patterns until closing time. Months later, customers began to request Sade's finishing because her stitches were neat and her buttonholes sat evenly. She still worked slowly, but she no longer mistook speed for skill. The shop taught her more than a trade. It showed her that embarrassment can become instruction when pride does not prevent learning.
question: Which of the following reflects the sequence of events in the passage?

options.A: Sade became skilful before she made any mistake
options.B: Sade spoiled cloth, received guided practice, and later gained customers' trust
options.C: Customers praised Sade, then she joined the tailoring shop
options.D: Madam Bisi closed the shop before Sade learnt patience

answer_text: Sade spoiled cloth, received guided practice, and later gained customers' trust

explanation.question_restate: We are to arrange the passage's ideas in the correct order.
explanation.principle: Sequence questions test whether you followed the unfolding of events from beginning to end.
steps: First, Sade joined the shop and discovered the discipline sewing required.
Then she spoiled an Ankara fabric by rushing.
Madam Bisi corrected her through practice.
Months later, customers requested Sade's finishing because her work had improved.
explanation.final_answer: B. Sade spoiled cloth, received guided practice, and later gained customers' trust
explanation.simple_explanation: Correct comprehension depends not only on facts, but also on when those facts occurred. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Figurative expressions as used in passages
passage_text: Sade joined Madam Bisi's tailoring shop because her family needed extra income, but she soon discovered that sewing demanded more discipline than she had imagined. Needles had to be arranged carefully, measurements recorded accurately and fabrics cut with patience rather than excitement. In her first week, she spoiled an expensive Ankara material by trimming the sleeve too short. Expecting a harsh rebuke, she stood silently beside the cutting table. Madam Bisi only asked her to explain what she had done and why she had rushed. Then she made Sade use a leftover piece of cloth to practise sleeve patterns until closing time. Months later, customers began to request Sade's finishing because her stitches were neat and her buttonholes sat evenly. She still worked slowly, but she no longer mistook speed for skill. The shop taught her more than a trade. It showed her that embarrassment can become instruction when pride does not prevent learning.
question: In the statement, "She no longer mistook speed for skill," the expression means that Sade had learnt that

options.A: working quickly is always better than working carefully
options.B: speed and skill are identical in tailoring
options.C: fast work without care is not the same as real mastery
options.D: slow workers can never become competent

answer_text: fast work without care is not the same as real mastery

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret a figurative or compressed expression within the passage.
explanation.principle: Figurative expressions often condense a lesson into a memorable contrast.
steps: The writer contrasts Sade's earlier rushing with her later patience and neat finishing.
So "mistook speed for skill" means she had wrongly assumed that quickness alone proved competence.
The passage teaches that careful accuracy matters more than mere haste.
The expression is figurative because it compares two things people often confuse.
explanation.final_answer: C. fast work without care is not the same as real mastery
explanation.simple_explanation: Real skill includes judgment, patience and precision, not just speed. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: Sade joined Madam Bisi's tailoring shop because her family needed extra income, but she soon discovered that sewing demanded more discipline than she had imagined. Needles had to be arranged carefully, measurements recorded accurately and fabrics cut with patience rather than excitement. In her first week, she spoiled an expensive Ankara material by trimming the sleeve too short. Expecting a harsh rebuke, she stood silently beside the cutting table. Madam Bisi only asked her to explain what she had done and why she had rushed. Then she made Sade use a leftover piece of cloth to practise sleeve patterns until closing time. Months later, customers began to request Sade's finishing because her stitches were neat and her buttonholes sat evenly. She still worked slowly, but she no longer mistook speed for skill. The shop taught her more than a trade. It showed her that embarrassment can become instruction when pride does not prevent learning.
question: Which lesson does Sade finally learn from her experience in the tailoring shop?

options.A: Embarrassment should always make a learner quit
options.B: Skill grows when mistakes are honestly corrected and practised through
options.C: Customers prefer sympathy to quality
options.D: Tailoring depends mainly on expensive fabric

answer_text: Skill grows when mistakes are honestly corrected and practised through

explanation.question_restate: We are to answer a general comprehension question on the passage's message.
explanation.principle: General comprehension asks you to understand the lesson conveyed by the full passage.
steps: Sade begins as a rushed beginner and makes a costly mistake.
Instead of being destroyed by shame, she is guided into practice.
Later she becomes known for neat finishing.
The closing sentence states that embarrassment can become instruction when pride does not prevent learning.
explanation.final_answer: B. Skill grows when mistakes are honestly corrected and practised through
explanation.simple_explanation: The passage teaches that humble correction and practice lead to growth. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: The principal announced that a neglected section of the school field would be turned into a vegetable garden. To some students, the plan sounded like punishment disguised as innovation. They imagined long afternoons under the sun while teachers pretended that manual labour was entertainment. The principal, however, insisted that the garden was meant to support the science lessons and supplement the school meal programme. During the first term, the agriculture club recorded plant growth, compared soils and observed how pests damaged leaves. The home economics unit later used part of the harvest in practical classes. Even students who disliked farming admitted that watching a seedling survive after weeks of care gave the subject a reality that textbooks lacked. The garden did not make everyone love agriculture, but it forced many to revise the lazy assumption that useful learning only happens behind a desk.
question: The central idea of the passage is that the school garden

options.A: was mainly a way of punishing lazy students
options.B: helped students see that useful learning can be practical
options.C: failed because textbooks were enough
options.D: produced more vegetables than the local market

answer_text: helped students see that useful learning can be practical

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main point the writer develops.
explanation.principle: The central theme usually appears through repeated emphasis and the final direction of the passage.
steps: Students first suspect the garden is disguised punishment.
The principal explains its academic and nutritional value.
Practical observation and harvest use later make the subject more real to students.
The passage ends by challenging the assumption that useful learning occurs only at desks.
explanation.final_answer: B. helped students see that useful learning can be practical
explanation.simple_explanation: The garden's deeper significance is its practical educational value. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: The principal announced that a neglected section of the school field would be turned into a vegetable garden. To some students, the plan sounded like punishment disguised as innovation. They imagined long afternoons under the sun while teachers pretended that manual labour was entertainment. The principal, however, insisted that the garden was meant to support the science lessons and supplement the school meal programme. During the first term, the agriculture club recorded plant growth, compared soils and observed how pests damaged leaves. The home economics unit later used part of the harvest in practical classes. Even students who disliked farming admitted that watching a seedling survive after weeks of care gave the subject a reality that textbooks lacked. The garden did not make everyone love agriculture, but it forced many to revise the lazy assumption that useful learning only happens behind a desk.
question: Which of the following is given as one use of the garden in the passage?

options.A: It was leased to traders after school hours
options.B: It supplied crops for home economics practical work
options.C: It was converted into a football training ground
options.D: It replaced all science laboratories in the school

answer_text: It supplied crops for home economics practical work

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a detail directly provided by the passage.
explanation.principle: Supporting details are specific examples that develop the main point.
steps: The passage says the home economics unit used part of the harvest in practical classes.
That is a clear, direct detail.
The other options are not stated and do not fit the passage.
In comprehension, watch for exact textual support rather than imaginative additions.
explanation.final_answer: B. It supplied crops for home economics practical work
explanation.simple_explanation: A careful reader spots the writer's concrete example and selects it. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: The principal announced that a neglected section of the school field would be turned into a vegetable garden. To some students, the plan sounded like punishment disguised as innovation. They imagined long afternoons under the sun while teachers pretended that manual labour was entertainment. The principal, however, insisted that the garden was meant to support the science lessons and supplement the school meal programme. During the first term, the agriculture club recorded plant growth, compared soils and observed how pests damaged leaves. The home economics unit later used part of the harvest in practical classes. Even students who disliked farming admitted that watching a seedling survive after weeks of care gave the subject a reality that textbooks lacked. The garden did not make everyone love agriculture, but it forced many to revise the lazy assumption that useful learning only happens behind a desk.
question: It may be inferred that some students originally opposed the garden because they

options.A: believed practical work had no place in schooling
options.B: were already expert farmers
options.C: wanted more science lessons outdoors
options.D: thought the principal disliked textbooks

answer_text: believed practical work had no place in schooling

explanation.question_restate: We are to infer an unstated belief from the students' reaction.
explanation.principle: Inference uses clues from attitude and response to uncover what the passage implies.
steps: The students saw the plan as punishment disguised as innovation.
The final sentence says the garden forced many to revise the assumption that useful learning only happens behind a desk.
This clearly implies that they had undervalued practical forms of learning.
Their objection grew from a narrow idea of what proper education looks like.
explanation.final_answer: A. believed practical work had no place in schooling
explanation.simple_explanation: The writer implies that some students equated schooling with classroom-only activity. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

subtopic: Contextual meaning of words and expressions
passage_text: The principal announced that a neglected section of the school field would be turned into a vegetable garden. To some students, the plan sounded like punishment disguised as innovation. They imagined long afternoons under the sun while teachers pretended that manual labour was entertainment. The principal, however, insisted that the garden was meant to support the science lessons and supplement the school meal programme. During the first term, the agriculture club recorded plant growth, compared soils and observed how pests damaged leaves. The home economics unit later used part of the harvest in practical classes. Even students who disliked farming admitted that watching a seedling survive after weeks of care gave the subject a reality that textbooks lacked. The garden did not make everyone love agriculture, but it forced many to revise the lazy assumption that useful learning only happens behind a desk.
question: As used in the passage, the phrase "behind a desk" refers chiefly to

options.A: administrative office work
options.B: formal classroom-style learning
options.C: secretarial duties
options.D: disciplinary hearings

answer_text: formal classroom-style learning

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret a phrase from its context.
explanation.principle: Contextual meaning often involves understanding a phrase beyond its literal surface.
steps: The passage contrasts the garden with textbook-based lessons.
Thus "behind a desk" stands for conventional classroom learning rather than literal furniture.
The writer uses the phrase symbolically to represent traditional school instruction.
The meaning is not office work or clerical duty here.
explanation.final_answer: B. formal classroom-style learning
explanation.simple_explanation: In comprehension, always connect the phrase to the surrounding contrast in ideas. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".

passage_text: The principal announced that a neglected section of the school field would be turned into a vegetable garden. To some students, the plan sounded like punishment disguised as innovation. They imagined long afternoons under the sun while teachers pretended that manual labour was entertainment. The principal, however, insisted that the garden was meant to support the science lessons and supplement the school meal programme. During the first term, the agriculture club recorded plant growth, compared soils and observed how pests damaged leaves. The home economics unit later used part of the harvest in practical classes. Even students who disliked farming admitted that watching a seedling survive after weeks of care gave the subject a reality that textbooks lacked. The garden did not make everyone love agriculture, but it forced many to revise the lazy assumption that useful learning only happens behind a desk.
question: The writer's tone toward the school garden project is

options.A: dismissive and sarcastic
options.B: balanced but supportive
options.C: angry and accusatory
options.D: frightened and uncertain

answer_text: balanced but supportive

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the writer's tone.
explanation.principle: Tone depends on how the writer frames both objections and outcomes.
steps: The writer acknowledges students' suspicion at the beginning.
Yet the later evidence shows educational value, and the conclusion supports the project without pretending everyone loved it.
That makes the tone supportive, but not exaggerated.
The writer is fair to the initial resistance while still endorsing the outcome.
explanation.final_answer: B. balanced but supportive
explanation.simple_explanation: Balanced approval is therefore the best description. For more guided practice and full lesson breakdowns, check our "Tutorial section".
